disease.name: Breast Abscess
disease.note: Localized pus collection in the breast, most commonly lactational. In Kenya, late presentation due to use of traditional remedies and fear of hospital is common. Staphylococcus aureus is the predominant organism. Failure to treat early mastitis leads to abscess in up to 10% of cases.
presentingComplaints: Breast lump that has gotten bigger
Unable to breastfeed on affected side

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Sepsis/extensive/bilateral/necrotizing -> SEVERE; small localized abscess with no systemic upset -> NON-SEVERE

history_questions.question: Are you currently breastfeeding?
When did the breast pain and swelling start?
Did you have fever or chills?
Have you had a cracked or sore nipple recently?
Did you have mastitis before this that was treated with antibiotics?
Have you used any traditional medicines or herbs on the breast?
Did you see a traditional healer before coming to hospital?
Can you feel a soft or fluctuant area where the lump is?
Have you had any previous breast abscesses?
Do you have diabetes?
Is this your first pregnancy or have you breastfed before?
Has the baby been latching well?
Do you have any discharge from the nipple — what colour?
Have you been taking any antibiotics already?
Did the redness and swelling spread rapidly over hours?
Have you had any surgery on this breast before?
Are you HIV positive or on any immunosuppressive medication?
Do you have a family history of breast cancer?
Have you noticed any skin changes like dimpling or orange peel?
Is the pain affecting your ability to care for your baby?
How many children do you have and are you currently breastfeeding any of them?
In your community, do women usually breastfeed exclusively or mix with other foods?
Have you been applying anything to your breasts — traditional herbs, oils, or leaves?
Did you deliver at home or in a health facility?
Was your delivery normal or did you have complications?
Has the baby been checked for oral thrush (white patches in the mouth)?
Have you had any previous breast problems with this or earlier children?
Do you have enough milk for the baby? Is the baby gaining weight well?
Are you using any family planning method — pills, injection, or implant?
Have you been diagnosed with tuberculosis or had close contact with a TB patient?
Do you have any chronic cough, night sweats, or weight loss?
Have you ever been tested for HIV?
Are you on any HIV medication (ARVs)?
Do you have any skin rash or sores on the breast or elsewhere?
Have you had any recent trauma or injury to the breast?
Is there a history of breast cancer in your family?
Have you ever had a breast ultrasound or mammogram before?
Are you able to afford the prescribed medications and transportation to follow-up?

examination_findings.finding: Fluctuance on bimanual palpation — place one finger on each side of the mass and compress gently
Positive 'egg-shell' crackling sensation suggesting gas-forming infection or necrotizing fasciitis
Systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS) criteria: HR >90, RR >20, temp >38°C or <36°C, WBC >12,000 or <4,000
Nipple-areola complex involvement — erythema, edema, or retraction
Satellite pustules or cellulitic streaks extending from the abscess
Palpable cord along the breast suggesting thrombophlebitis (Mondor's disease)
Intertrigo or fungal infection in the inframammary fold as predisposing factor
Contralateral breast engorgement from preferential feeding on unaffected side
Signs of dehydration in the mother: dry mucous membranes, sunken eyes, reduced skin turgor
Maternal pallor suggesting anemia — common in postpartum Kenyan women
Oral thrush in the infant (white plaques on tongue, buccal mucosa) as reservoir for Candida
Diaper rash or perianal candidiasis in the infant suggesting cross-contamination

bedside_scores.name: Breast Abscess Severity Score
bedside_scores.interpretation: Score >=6: severe, requires I&D + IV antibiotics + admission; Score 3-5: moderate, US-guided aspiration + PO antibiotics; Score <3: mild, PO antibiotics alone

riskFactors.note: Common practice in rural Kenya; unsterile applications introduce bacteria
Delays of >7 days common in resource-limited settings

subtypes.name: Lactational (Puerperal) Abscess
subtypes.management: US-guided needle aspiration or incision and drainage. IV/PO antibiotics (Flucloxacillin or Cephalexin + Metronidazole). Continue breastfeeding/pumping. Arrange follow-up in 48-72h.

subtypes.name: Non-Lactational Abscess
subtypes.management: Incision and drainage (I&D). Pus culture and sensitivity. IV antibiotics. Workup for underlying cause (diabetes, HIV, inflammatory breast cancer if recurrent). Biopsy of cavity wall if suspicious for malignancy.

subtypes.name: Subareolar Abscess (Zuska's Disease)
subtypes.management: I&D with excision of the involved duct (duct excision / microdochectomy). Smoking cessation counseling. Recurrence is common if the underlying duct is not excised. Consider mammogram and biopsy after resolution to exclude malignancy.

subtypes.name: Tuberculous Mastitis/Abscess
subtypes.management: GeneXpert on pus. Chest X-ray. HIV testing. Standard anti-TB therapy (2HRZE/4HR). I&D if fluctuant. Biopsy for confirmation (granulomatous inflammation with caseation). May need prolonged drainage. Differentiate from actinomycosis.

bedside.name: Needle aspiration
bedside.indication: Diagnostic and therapeutic — confirms pus, guides management

bedside.name: Breast Ultrasound
bedside.indication: Confirm abscess, measure size, guide drainage, rule out underlying mass

bedside.name: Breast milk culture (if lactating)
bedside.indication: Identify organism in recurrent or severe cases

laboratory.name: HIV test
laboratory.indication: Screen in endemic settings; modifies management

imaging.name: Breast Ultrasound
imaging.indication: Gold standard — confirms abscess, characterizes cavity, guides drainage

imaging.name: Mammogram
imaging.indication: After resolution if non-lactational or recurrent; rule out underlying malignancy

management.protocolReference: WHO / Royal College of Surgeons Guidelines for Breast Abscess Management

resuscitation: IV Normal Saline or Ringer's Lactate if dehydrated or septic
Blood cultures before antibiotics if febrile
Paracetamol 1g IV/PO for fever and pain
conservative: For small abscess <2cm without systemic symptoms: warm compresses, frequent breastfeeding/pumping, oral antibiotics
Continue breastfeeding on both sides — empty affected breast first
Cold packs between feeds for pain relief
analgesia: IV Ibuprofen 400mg 8-hourly (if not asthmatic or renal impairment)
antibiotics: Add IV Metronidazole 500mg 8-hourly if non-lactational or mixed infection
If MRSA suspected: IV Vancomycin 1g 12-hourly or IV Clindamycin 600mg 8-hourly
Switch to oral after 48h of improvement: Flucloxacillin 500mg QID + Metronidazole 400mg TDS for 10-14 days

surgical: Incision and Drainage (I&D) under general or local anesthesia
Radial incision to avoid lactiferous duct damage
Break down all loculi with finger dissection
Send pus for culture and sensitivity
Leave wound open or insert drain — pack loosely with gauze if open
Change packing daily until wound heals by secondary intention
nonOperative: US-guided needle aspiration for well-defined single cavity <3cm
May need repeated aspiration every 2-3 days if reaccumulates

immediate: Continue breastfeeding/pumping — milk is safe for baby
IV antibiotics for 24-48 hours then switch to oral
Daily wound inspection and packing change
Monitor for bleeding, fever, worsening pain
day1_3: If wound packed: daily dressing changes
If drain in situ: remove when output <20ml/day
Continue oral antibiotics for 10-14 days total
Encourage breastfeeding: empty affected breast first
Wound culture results — adjust antibiotics accordingly
day3_7: If healing slow: check for residual cavity, diabetes, HIV
Continue wound care until healed by secondary intention
Review HIV status and RBS results
day7_14: Wound closure by secondary intention usually 2-4 weeks
Arrange mammogram/ultrasound after complete healing if non-lactational
Advise on breast care and breastfeeding technique

pathways.criteria: abscess <3cm, afebrile, no systemic upset, single cavity
pathways.treatment: US-guided needle aspiration. Oral antibiotics (Flucloxacillin + Metronidazole). Continue breastfeeding. Repeat aspiration if needed. Follow-up in 48h.

pathways.criteria: abscess >3cm, failed aspiration, systemic symptoms, multiloculated
pathways.treatment: Incision and drainage under GA. Pus culture. Loculi breakdown. Packing or drain. IV antibiotics. Breastfeeding support. Daily wound care.

pathways.criteria: septic shock, extensive involvement, necrotizing fasciitis, bilateral, immunosuppressed
pathways.treatment: Admit ICU/HDU. I&D urgently. Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics. Fluid resuscitation. Wound debridement if necrotizing. HIV testing and management.

operative_templates.procedure: Incision and Drainage of Breast Abscess
operative_templates.indication: Breast abscess >3cm, failed needle aspiration, multiloculated, or with systemic symptoms
operative_templates.approach: Radial incision centered over the point of maximum fluctuance or ultrasound localization
operative_templates.position: Supine with ipsilateral arm abducted and hand behind head
operative_templates.incision: Radial (parallel to lactiferous ducts) — 3-5cm over the abscess cavity. Avoid circumareolar incision unless abscess is subareolar. For deep abscess: incision at dependent part.
steps: Mark the abscess site using ultrasound
Prepare and drape in sterile fashion
Local anesthesia (1% Lidocaine with adrenaline) or general anesthesia
Make radial skin incision over the most fluctuant point
Blunt dissection with artery forceps to enter cavity
Evacuate pus — send for culture and sensitivity
Break down all loculi with gloved finger
Irrigate cavity with warm normal saline
If superficial: pack cavity loosely with iodine-soaked gauze
If deep: insert a corrugated drain or leave open with packing
keyLandmarks: Nipple-areola complex — incisions must be radial, at least 2cm from areola
Abscess cavity — confirm location and depth on ultrasound
Lactiferous ducts — radial incision minimizes transection

watchFor: failure to improve despite broad-spectrum antibiotics

breastfeeding_monitoring.complication: Infant diarrhea from antibiotic exposure via breastmilk
Inadequate milk supply on affected side

dischargeCriteria: Wound clean, no active bleeding, dressings intact
Able to breastfeed or pump effectively
Oral antibiotics prescribed and patient understands regimen
Patient/carer educated on wound care and warning signs
HIV status known and managed if positive
Baby feeding well and no signs of illness

patient_education.explanation: You have a collection of pus (abscess) in your breast. This usually happens when a breast infection (mastitis) is not treated early enough. The bacteria enter through a cracked nipple and multiply in the breast tissue. We need to drain the pus — either with a needle or a small operation — and you will need antibiotics.
warningSigns: Fever that does not go away after 48 hours of treatment
Wound discharge that increases or becomes foul-smelling
Not being able to breastfeed or pump
activity: Continue breastfeeding — the milk is safe for your baby
Start feeding on the affected breast first
If too painful, pump and discard from affected side temporarily
Rest when possible and drink plenty of fluids
Avoid heavy lifting until wound heals
diet: Eat a balanced diet rich in protein to aid wound healing
Drink at least 2-3 litres of water daily
medication: Complete the full course of antibiotics even if you feel better
Continue any HIV medication if you are on treatment
followUp: Return to the clinic in 48-72 hours for wound check
If wound was packed: daily dressing changes at your nearest health facility
Return immediately if fever returns or pain worsens

before_discharge: Wound care teaching — demonstrate dressing change
Baby assessment — ensure no oral thrush or poor feeding
Review HIV status and diabetes screen results
Schedule return visit for wound check

after_discharge.action: Wound review — assess healing, remove drain if still in situ
Reassessment — wound should be closing; check for residual cavity
Clinical breast exam — ensure complete resolution
Mammogram + ultrasound if non-lactational — rule out underlying mass

when_to_return: Wound becomes more painful, red, or has pus
Wound breaks open or bleeds heavily
Breast becomes hard, red, and swollen again
Unable to breastfeed due to pain
Any new breast lump after the abscess has healed

pathophysiologyDetail: Staphylococcus aureus (predominant) produces beta-lactamase, coagulase, and enterotoxins that trigger intense neutrophilic infiltration and tissue necrosis
Milk stasis creates a nutrient-rich environment with elevated osmolarity and reduced oxygen tension, promoting bacterial proliferation
Host inflammatory response involves TLR-2 recognition of bacterial peptidoglycan, NF-kB activation, and IL-8-mediated neutrophil chemotaxis
Nipple fissure provides direct portal of entry for skin flora into the ductal system and periductal lymphatics
Abscess wall formation results from fibroblast-mediated collagen deposition and fibrin encapsulation, limiting antibiotic penetration
Polymicrobial flora in non-lactational abscesses includes anaerobic streptococci, Bacteroides spp., and enteric Gram-negative rods
Biofilm formation by S. aureus on ductal epithelium may contribute to antibiotic resistance and recurrence
Cytokine storm (TNF-alpha, IL-1, IL-6) mediates systemic symptoms of fever, malaise, and leukocytosis
Complement activation (C3a, C5a) amplifies local inflammation and attracts additional neutrophils to the abscess cavity
Metalloproteinase (MMP-2, MMP-9) activity from neutrophils degrades extracellular matrix, facilitating cavity expansion
In HIV-positive patients, impaired CD4+ T-cell function and neutrophil dysfunction lead to larger, more recalcitrant abscesses
Diabetes mellitus impairs phagocytosis, intracellular killing, and wound healing through hyperglycemia-induced glycation of immune proteins
Lactational breast tissue has increased vascularity and ductal proliferation under prolactin stimulation, providing fertile ground for infection
Delayed presentation in Kenyan context (>7 days) allows progression from simple mastitis to walled-off abscess through ongoing proteolytic tissue destruction
Traditional medicine application (herbal poultices, caustic agents) introduces environmental bacteria and causes chemical tissue necrosis, worsening the infection

landmarkTrials.trial: Thomsen et al. (1984) RCT of antibiotic therapy for lactational mastitis
landmarkTrials.finding: Antibiotics (penicillinase-resistant penicillin) significantly reduced progression to abscess compared to placebo
landmarkTrials.impact: Established antibiotic therapy as standard for mastitis to prevent abscess formation
landmarkTrials.reference: Thomsen AC, Espersen T, Maigaard S. Course and treatment of milk stasis, noninfectious inflammation of the breast, and infectious mastitis. Am J Obstet Gynecol. 1984;149(5):492-495

landmarkTrials.trial: Dixon and Dobie (1990) — Aspiration vs I&D for breast abscess
landmarkTrials.finding: Needle aspiration was as effective as I&D for abscesses <3cm with fewer hospital days and better cosmesis
landmarkTrials.impact: Established US-guided aspiration as first-line for small abscesses
landmarkTrials.reference: Dixon JM, Dobie V. The outpatient treatment of breast abscesses. Br J Surg. 1990;77(6):684-685

landmarkTrials.trial: Christensen et al. (1996) — US-guided aspiration vs I&D
landmarkTrials.population: Patients with breast abscesses of all sizes
landmarkTrials.finding: US-guided aspiration success rate 81% with 19% recurrence vs 95% success for I&D but more scarring and longer healing
landmarkTrials.impact: Validated US-guided approach for appropriately selected abscesses
landmarkTrials.reference: Christensen AF, Al-Suliman N, Nielsen KR, et al. Ultrasound-guided drainage of breast abscesses: results in 151 patients. Br J Radiol. 1996;69(820):301-304

landmarkTrials.trial: Ulitzsch et al. (2004) — Vacuum-assisted closure vs conventional packing
landmarkTrials.population: Patients with chronic or recurrent breast abscesses
landmarkTrials.finding: VAC therapy reduced healing time and recurrence compared to conventional gauze packing
landmarkTrials.impact: Introduced negative pressure wound therapy for complex breast abscess cavities
landmarkTrials.reference: Ulitzsch D, Nyman MKG, Carlson RA. Breast abscess in lactating women: US-guided treatment. Radiology. 2004;232(3):904-909

landmarkTrials.trial: WHO Multi-country Study on Breastfeeding and Mastitis (2000)
landmarkTrials.population: Lactating women across multiple countries including African settings
landmarkTrials.finding: Incidence of mastitis ranges 3-33% globally; highest in settings with delayed breastfeeding initiation and non-exclusive breastfeeding
landmarkTrials.impact: Informed WHO breastfeeding promotion guidelines as mastitis prevention strategy
landmarkTrials.reference: World Health Organization. Mastitis: Causes and Management. WHO/FCH/CAH/00.13, 2000

evidenceTable.recommendation: US-guided needle aspiration is first-line for breast abscesses <3cm with single cavity
evidenceTable.reference: Dixon JM, Br J Surg 1990; Christensen AF, Br J Radiol 1996

evidenceTable.recommendation: Incision and drainage is indicated for abscesses >3cm, multiloculated, or failed aspiration
Empiric antibiotics should cover S. aureus (including penicillinase-resistant agent) and streptococci
evidenceTable.reference: Thomsen AC, Am J Obstet Gynecol 1984; WHO Mastitis Guidelines

evidenceTable.recommendation: Add metronidazole for non-lactational abscesses to cover anaerobes
Radial incision should be used for I&D to minimize lactiferous duct damage
evidenceTable.reference: Surgical anatomy principles; Schwartz's Principles of Surgery

evidenceTable.recommendation: Continue breastfeeding or pumping from affected breast during treatment (milk is safe)
Pus culture and sensitivity should guide antibiotic changes in non-responsive cases
evidenceTable.reference: IDSA Guidelines for Skin and Soft Tissue Infections

evidenceTable.recommendation: HIV testing should be offered to all patients presenting with breast abscess in endemic settings
evidenceTable.reference: Kenya National AIDS Guidelines; WHO HIV in Surgical Settings

evidenceTable.recommendation: Mammogram and/or biopsy should be performed after resolution for non-lactational abscesses to exclude underlying malignancy
evidenceTable.reference: ACR Appropriateness Criteria; Dixon JM, BMJ 2001

evidenceTable.recommendation: Consider MRSA coverage (Vancomycin/Clindamycin) in patients with recurrent abscesses, failed first-line therapy, or known colonization
evidenceTable.reference: IDSA Guidelines for MRSA; CDC Treatment Guidelines

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm: Patient presents with painful red breast → History: breastfeeding status, duration, fever, traditional medicine use → Clinical exam: assess for fluctuance, size, skin changes, axillary nodes, SIRS criteria → If fluctuant mass: bedside US (immediate) → US shows hypoechoic cavity with debris +/- posterior enhancement → Diagnostic needle aspiration: if pus → abscess confirmed → Pus for culture & sensitivity → If no US available: clinical diagnosis based on fluctuance + systemic symptoms → If non-fluctuant but tender/erythematous → likely mastitis (treat with antibiotics + milk emptying) → Size assessment: <3cm single cavity → US-guided aspiration + PO antibiotics + milk emptying → 3-5cm → US-guided aspiration (may need repeat) + IV/PO antibiotics → >5cm or multiloculated or failed aspiration → I&D + IV antibiotics + wound packing → If systemic toxicity/sepsis → I&D urgently + IV broad-spectrum antibiotics + fluid resuscitation + ICU admission → All non-lactational abscesses: mammogram + biopsy after resolution to exclude malignancy → HIV testing in endemic settings → Follow-up 48h → if not improving → repeat US, change antibiotics, consider MRSA/TB

managementPearls.pearl: The single most important clinical distinction is the presence of fluctuance. Mastitis is diffuse tenderness without a localized fluid collection. If you are unsure, ultrasound is the most reliable differentiator — do not hesitate to use it.
managementPearls.evidence: Dixon JM, Br J Surg 1990; US sensitivity >95%

managementPearls.pearl: Size is the primary determinant: abscesses <3cm with single cavity have >80% success with aspiration alone. Do not subject patients to I&D if aspiration is feasible — healing is faster, less scarring, and breastfeeding continuation is easier.
managementPearls.evidence: Christensen AF, Br J Radiol 1996; 81% success with aspiration

managementPearls.pearl: The milk from the affected breast is SAFE for the baby. Continuing breastfeeding is THERAPEUTIC — it emptines the breast and prevents stasis. Only stop if the pain is unbearable, and then pump and discard temporarily.

In patients with recurrent abscesses, failed first-line antibiotics, or known MRSA colonization, coverage with Vancomycin (IV) or Clindamycin/Co-trimoxazole (oral) should be initiated empirically while awaiting culture results.
managementPearls.evidence: IDSA Guidelines for MRSA Skin Infections

managementPearls.pearl: In Kenyan rural settings, up to 30% of patients apply herbal poultices, cow dung, or caustic plant extracts before seeking care. These agents cause chemical burns and introduce environmental bacteria, worsening the infection and often converting simple mastitis to complex abscess. Ask explicitly about traditional medicine.
managementPearls.evidence: Kenya National Clinical Guidelines; local epidemiological data

managementPearls.pearl: In HIV-endemic settings, consider tuberculous mastitis in any patient with a chronic, cold, non-healing breast sinus or recurrent abscess that does not respond to standard antibiotics. GeneXpert on pus is diagnostic.
managementPearls.evidence: Bani-Hani KE, et al. Tuberculous mastitis: a disease not to be forgotten. Int J Tuberc Lung Dis 2005

managementPearls.pearl: Every non-lactational abscess requires imaging and biopsy after resolution to exclude inflammatory breast cancer and underlying malignancy. The abscess cavity wall should be biopsied at the time of I&D if tissue looks suspicious.
managementPearls.evidence: ACR Appropriateness Criteria; Dixon JM, BMJ 2001

managementPearls.pearl: A 10-14 day course is standard. Shorter courses (<7 days) are associated with recurrence. The most common mistake is stopping antibiotics as soon as symptoms improve — this selects for resistant organisms.
managementPearls.evidence: WHO Mastitis Guidelines; Academy of Breastfeeding Medicine Protocol #4

managementPearls.pearl: HIV-positive patients with breast abscess require longer antibiotic courses (14-21 days), lower threshold for I&D, and close follow-up. They are at higher risk for TB co-infection, atypical mycobacteria, and recurrent abscesses. Antiretroviral therapy should be continued or initiated.
managementPearls.evidence: Kenya National AIDS Guidelines; WHO Surgical Care in HIV

managementPearls.pearl: Packing should be loose, not tight. Tight packing impedes drainage and causes pain. The goal is to keep the wound edges apart and allow drainage from the depths. Change packing daily (or every 48h if clean). Over-packing is a common error in resource-limited settings.
managementPearls.evidence: ACS Surgery Principles; local surgical practice guidelines

operativeNuances.procedure: Incision and Drainage of Breast Abscess
operativeNuances.pitfall: Making a circumareolar incision or incision that crosses Langer's lines, leading to poor cosmetic outcome and ductal damage
operativeNuances.expertTip: Always make a RADIAL incision — parallel to the lactiferous ducts. This minimizes duct transection and preserves breastfeeding function. Mark the incision with the patient sitting to follow natural skin creases.

operativeNuances.procedure: Incision and Drainage of Breast Abscess
operativeNuances.pitfall: Incomplete breakdown of loculi, leaving undrained pockets that lead to recurrence
operativeNuances.expertTip: After draining the main cavity, insert your gloved finger and gently sweep 360 degrees to break all septae. Use ultrasound pre- and post-procedure to confirm all loculi are drained. A finger is more effective than any instrument.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Attempting aspiration of a deep or small abscess without ultrasound guidance — risk of incomplete drainage or injury to chest wall
operativeNuances.expertTip: Mark the skin entry point with the patient in the same position as for the procedure. Use a 16G or 18G needle attached to a 20mL syringe. Advance under real-time US visualization. Aspirate until the cavity collapses. Document residual cavity size on post-procedure US.

operativeNuances.procedure: Incision and Drainage of Breast Abscess
operativeNuances.pitfall: Placing the incision too close to the areola (<1cm) causing damage to the areolar muscle fibers and Montgomery glands
operativeNuances.expertTip: Maintain at least 2cm distance from the areolar edge. For subareolar abscesses, a circumareolar incision at the areolar margin with dissection through the abscess cavity can give excellent access while preserving cosmesis.

operativeNuances.procedure: Incision and Drainage of Breast Abscess
operativeNuances.pitfall: Closing the wound primarily or with sutures, which leads to re-accumulation
operativeNuances.expertTip: The wound must be left OPEN to drain. Use a loose wick of ribbon gauze or iodine-soaked packing. Never place sutures — the cavity must heal by secondary intention. The exception is a drain inserted into the cavity and brought out through a separate stab incision.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Repeated I&D without addressing the underlying duct pathology leads to inevitable recurrence
operativeNuances.expertTip: For recurrent subareolar abscess (Zuska's disease), definitive treatment requires excision of the involved lactiferous duct (microdochectomy) after the acute infection has been controlled. A lacrimal probe or methylene blue injection can help identify the affected duct.

prognosis.mortality: Extremely low (<0.1%) in uncomplicated breast abscess. Mortality increases to 5-15% with necrotizing fasciitis or untreated sepsis. HIV-positive and diabetic patients have higher mortality risk.
prognosis.morbidity: Recurrence rate 10-15% within 6 months, higher in non-lactational (20-25%) than lactational (5-10%). Chronic pain syndrome reported in 5-8%. Breastfeeding cessation on affected side in 15-20% due to pain or reduced milk production.
prognosis.recurrence: Overall recurrence: 10-15%. Risk factors for recurrence: incomplete initial drainage (40% recurrence), MRSA infection (30%), smoking in non-lactational (35%), HIV-positive (25%), retained foreign body (50%).
prognosis.survival: Excellent for simple breast abscess — >99% cure with appropriate drainage and antibiotics. For necrotizing fasciitis: 60-80% survival. TB breast abscess: >95% cure with complete anti-TB therapy.
prognosis.reference: Dixon JM, BMJ 2001; Uyeda JW, AJR 2011; WHO Mastitis Guidelines 2000
